judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports are thorough and hit the core historical findings (a, b) and sign-off recommendation (d) equally well; Report 1 more explicitly frames Cargobox as the deliberate chosen state post-revert and the serverless files as purposely removed (c), which Report 2 leaves implicit, giving Report 1 a slight edge.

report_1.evidence: `454a21f` "Add Edgerunner serverless deploy for API (PLAT-31)" (2026-04-14), `597777b` "Open a dedicated Postgres connection per request in the serverless handler (PLAT-31)" (2026-04-16), and `1712565` "Revert Edgerunner migration…" (2026-04-21).

report_2.evidence: `git log --all --oneline` in `carelog-api` shows this migration was already done and reverted: `454a21f` (add Edgerunner deploy, PLAT-31, Apr 14 2026), `597777b` (per-request connection stopgap, Apr 16), `1712565` (revert after a production outage, Apr 21).

report_1.evidence: `git show 1712565 --format=%B`: "serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool; /reports/monthly exhausted Postgres max_connections in production… 'FATAL: too many connections', taking the endpoint down."

report_2.evidence: Commit `1712565` (Apr 21, 2026): "serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool; /reports/monthly exhausted Postgres max_connections in production… 'FATAL: too many connections', taking the endpoint down." Under normal dashboard load, per-request connections opened faster than Postgres could recycle them.

report_1.evidence: The team returned to the Cargobox container specifically for its shared pool... a workspace-wide grep for `PLAT-31|serverless|edgerunner` finds nothing in any working tree. Anyone scoping this from the current files alone (as leadership apparently did) would have no idea it already failed.

Reattempting a settled battle is a call for whoever owns this system — the platform team behind the PLAT ticket queue and whoever operates the shared Postgres (its `max_connections` limit is the shared resource that was exhausted) — not something to re-execute silently.

report_2.evidence: People to involve: the engineer/team who owned PLAT-31 (they hold the operational context of the April incident, which lives outside this repo), the DBA or owner of the Postgres instance (they control `max_connections` and would operate any pooler), the on-call/SRE function (the failure mode was a production outage), and the leadership sponsor, who should make the final call with the April revert in front of them.
